message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Whole-corpus 'stability across resamples' is measured against the null median, not the observed statistic — resamples disconnected from the observed effect earn stability=1.0
DIMENSION: methodology integrity
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py 179-189 (_resample_stability) and 265 (call site passing median(null_distribution))
DESCRIPTION: confirm_whole_corpus computes stability as `_resample_stability(resample_statistics, median(null_distribution), direction)` — the fraction of resamples on the predicted side of the NULL MEDIAN, not of the OBSERVED statistic. The docstring claims this measures 'the effect direction reproduces across resamples' and gates SUPPORTED on it (audit pass-1 issue #21 made stability engine-measured rather than caller-asserted, but used the wrong reference point). Because the reference is the null center, resamples that barely exceed the null median count as fully 'stable' even when they are nowhere near the observed effect — so 'the observed effect reproduces' is not what is actually tested. CONCRETE PoC: null=range(0,21) (median 10), observed=100 (significant, p_support=0.045), resamples=[10.0001]*100 (just above the null median, far below the observed 100). confirm_whole_corpus returns SUPPORTED with stability=1.0, despite the resamples reproducing nothing of the size-100 effect. The existing tests only use resamples at +/-100 (far from the null), so the flaw is never exercised. This weakens (does not fully defeat — resamples must still land on the predicted side) the stability guard that gates the SUPPORTED verdict.
SUGGESTED FIX: Measure stability relative to the observed statistic's relationship to the null, not the null median alone — e.g. compute each resample's own empirical p (or sign of effect vs the null) and require the fraction that remain significant/extreme in the predicted tail to meet stability_threshold, so 'stability' reflects reproduction of the observed effect, not merely being on the predicted side of the null center.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	from statistics import median
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        require_locked(hypothesis)
55	
56	
57	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
58	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
59	
60	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
61	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
62	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
63	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
64	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
65	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
66	    """
67	    if not hypothesis.locked:
68	        raise FirewallViolation(
69	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
70	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
71	        )
72	
73	
74	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
75	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
76	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
77	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
78	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
79	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
80	
81	
82	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
83	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
84	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
85	
86	
87	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
88	    if not math.isfinite(value):
89	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
90	
91	
92	def verdict_from_pvalue(
93	    hypothesis_id: str,
94	    *,
95	    statistic: float,
96	    p_value: float,
97	    alpha: float,
98	    powered: bool,
99	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
100	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
101	) -> Verdict:
102	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
103	
104	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
105	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
106	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
107	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
108	
109	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
110	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
111	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
112	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
113	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
114	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
115	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
116	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
117	    """
118	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
119	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
120	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
121	
122	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
123	    common = dict(
124	        statistic=statistic,
125	        threshold=alpha,
126	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
127	    )
128	    if not powered:
129	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
130	    if p_value <= alpha:
131	        if direction_supports_claim:
132	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
133	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
134	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
135	
136	
137	def confirm_unit_level(
138	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
139	    *,
140	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
141	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
142	    statistic: float,
143	    p_value: float,
144	    alpha: float,
145	    powered: bool,
146	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
147	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
148	) -> Verdict:
149	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
150	
151	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
152	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
153	    """
154	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
155	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
156	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
157	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
158	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
159	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
160	        statistic=statistic,
161	        p_value=p_value,
162	        alpha=alpha,
163	        powered=powered,
164	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
165	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
166	    )
167	
168	
169	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
170	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
171	    n = len(null)
172	    if tail == "greater":
173	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
174	    else:
175	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
176	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
177	
178	
179	def _resample_stability(resamples: Sequence[float], reference: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
180	    """Fraction of resampled statistics that fall on the predicted side of ``reference``.
181	
182	    A measured stand-in for "the effect direction reproduces across resamples": the closer
183	    to 1.0, the more consistently the resampled statistic stays in the predicted tail.
184	    """
185	    if tail == "greater":
186	        agree = sum(1 for r in resamples if r > reference)
187	    else:
188	        agree = sum(1 for r in resamples if r < reference)
189	    return agree / len(resamples)
190	
191	
192	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
193	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
194	    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one."""
195	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
196	    if locked_dir is not None:
197	        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
198	            raise ValueError(
199	                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
200	                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
201	            )
202	        return locked_dir
203	    if predicted_direction is None:
204	        raise ValueError(
205	            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
206	            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
207	        )
208	    return predicted_direction
209	
210	
211	def confirm_whole_corpus(
212	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
213	    *,
214	    observed: float,
215	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
216	    alpha: float,
217	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
218	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
219	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
220	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
221	) -> Verdict:
222	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
223	
224	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
225	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
226	
227	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
228	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
229	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
230	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
231	
232	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
233	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
234	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
235	
236	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
237	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
238	    """
239	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
240	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
241	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
242	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
243	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
244	    if not null_distribution:
245	        raise ValueError(
246	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
247	        )
248	    for v in null_distribution:
249	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
250	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
251	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
252	
253	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
254	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
255	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
256	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
257	    n = len(null_distribution)
258	
259	    stability: float | None = None
260	    if resample_statistics is not None:
261	        if not resample_statistics:
262	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
263	        for r in resample_statistics:
264	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
265	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, median(null_distribution), direction)
266	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
267	
268	    rule = (
269	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
270	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
271	    )
272	    evidence = {
273	        "p_support": p_support,
274	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
275	        "null_n": n,
276	        "predicted_direction": direction,
277	        "stability": stability,
278	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
279	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
280	    }
281	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
282	
283	    # A contradiction (significant in the opposite tail) is reportable without stability.
284	    if p_contra <= alpha:
285	        return Verdict.contradicted(
286	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
287	        )
288	    if stability is None:
289	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
290	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
291	            rule,
292	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
293	            **common,
294	        )
295	    if not stable:
296	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
297	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
298	        )
299	    if p_support <= alpha:
300	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
301	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
302	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
303	    )
304

content.text: The claim is accurate about the code: line 265 passes `median(null_distribution)` as the reference to `_resample_stability`. Let me verify the PoC empirically.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && cat > /tmp/poc_stability.py << 'EOF'
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "src")
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus, _empirical_p, _resample_stability
from statistics import median

# Inspect Hypothesis / HypothesisKind / Direction usage
from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind

# Build a locked whole-corpus hypothesis with predicted_direction greater
import inspect
print("Hypothesis signature:", inspect.signature(Hypothesis.__init__))
print("fields:", [f for f in dir(Hypothesis) if not f.startswith("_")])
EOF
.venv/bin/python /tmp/poc_stability.py

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && cat > /tmp/poc2.py << 'EOF'
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "src")
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus, _empirical_p
from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
from statistics import median

print("HypothesisOrigin:", list(HypothesisOrigin))

h = Hypothesis(
    hypothesis_id="H1",
    statement="effect is large",
    kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,
    origin=list(HypothesisOrigin)[0],
    test_name="perm",
    decision_rule="p<alpha + stable",
    predicted_direction="greater",
    locked_at="2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00",
    timestamp_proof="proof",
)
print("locked?", h.locked)

null = list(range(0, 21))   # median 10
observed = 100
resamples = [10.0001]*100   # just above null median, nowhere near observed 100

print("p_support =", _empirical_p(null, observed, "greater"))
v = confirm_whole_corpus(
    h, observed=observed, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05,
    resample_statistics=resamples, stability_threshold=0.9,
)
print("verdict:", v.outcome if hasattr(v,'outcome') else v)
print("stability:", v.evidence.get("stability"))
EOF
.venv/bin/python /tmp/poc2.py

content.content:
HypothesisOrigin: [<HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY: 'discovery'>, <HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM: 'external_claim'>]
locked? True
p_support = 0.045454545454545456
verdict: Verdict(hypothesis_id='H1', label=<VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED: 'supported'>, decision_rule="empirical p from 21-sample null at alpha=0.05, predicted tail 'greater'; stability across resamples >= 0.9 (engine-measured)", statistic=100, threshold=0.05, evidence=FrozenDict({'p_support': 0.045454545454545456, 'p_contradict': 1.0, 'null_n': 21, 'predicted_direction': 'greater', 'stability': 1.0, 'stability_threshold': 0.9, 'n_resamples': 100}), notes='')
stability: 1.0

content.text:
The PoC reproduces exactly as claimed: SUPPORTED with stability=1.0, despite resamples (10.0001) being nowhere near the observed effect of 100. The reference point is the null median, not the observed statistic.

Let me assess the severity by considering what the "right" reference would be and whether the current behavior is genuinely weaker. Let me check the suggested-fix direction and whether stability vs observed would change the verdict.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && cat > /tmp/poc3.py << 'EOF'
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "src")
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import _resample_stability, _empirical_p
from statistics import median

null = list(range(0, 21))   # median 10
observed = 100
resamples = [10.0001]*100

# Current: reference = null median
print("stability vs null median:", _resample_stability(resamples, median(null), "greater"))

# Suggested fix variant A: stability vs observed statistic
print("stability vs observed:", _resample_stability(resamples, observed, "greater"))

# Suggested fix variant B: fraction of resamples that remain significant in predicted tail
sig = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, "greater") <= 0.05) / len(resamples)
print("fraction of resamples significant at 0.05:", sig)

# Sanity: a genuine reproduction (resamples near observed) still passes either way
good = [95, 102, 110, 98, 103]*20
print("good vs null median:", _resample_stability(good, median(null), "greater"))
print("good vs observed:", _resample_stability(good, observed, "greater"))
EOF
.venv/bin/python /tmp/poc3.py

content.text:
The tests confirm the claim: `_STABLE_HI = [100.0]*20` and `_STABLE_LO = [-100.0]*20` — resamples are always far from the null, never in the "barely past null median but far from observed" regime. The flaw is never exercised.

I have enough to make the determination. The claim is accurate on all points:

1. Code fact: line 265 uses `median(null_distribution)` as the reference, not `observed`. Verified.
2. PoC: reproduces exactly — SUPPORTED, stability=1.0 with resamples nowhere near the observed effect. Verified.
3. Docstring claim ("the effect direction reproduces across resamples") is stronger than what's measured. Verified.
4. Tests never exercise it. Verified.

Now severity calibration. The guard is genuinely weakened but not defeated: resamples must still land on the predicted side of the null center, so a sign-flip is still caught. It's a real methodology-integrity defect (the SUPPORTED gate is supposed to verify reproduction of the observed effect, and it doesn't), but the practical exploit requires resamples that are pathologically clustered just past the null median — which in legitimate use (resamples are draws of the same statistic that produced `observed`) would normally be near `observed`. It's a real defect that weakens a gate, MEDIUM is appropriate.

input.reasoning:
CONFIRMED TRUE production-readiness defect (methodology integrity). The assessor's claim is accurate in every checkable particular.

Code fact: in confirm_whole_corpus (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:265) stability is computed as `_resample_stability(resample_statistics, median(null_distribution), direction)`. _resample_stability (lines 179-189) returns the fraction of resamples strictly on the predicted side of the supplied reference. The reference passed is the NULL MEDIAN, not the OBSERVED statistic. So "stability" measures only that resamples land on the predicted side of the null center — not that they reproduce the observed effect.

Why this matters: stability is the second gate on the SUPPORTED verdict (lines 295-300: must be stable AND p_support<=alpha). The docstring (lines 222-234) and _resample_stability's own docstring claim it measures that "the effect direction reproduces across resamples". It does not — a resample that barely clears the null median but is nowhere near the observed effect still counts as fully stable. Pass-1 issue #21 made stability engine-measured rather than caller-asserted (good), but anchored it to the wrong reference point, so the intended guarantee is not delivered.

Severity = MEDIUM (not higher): the guard is weakened, not defeated. Resamples must still land on the predicted side of the null center, so a genuine contradiction / sign flip is still caught, and the independent p_contra check (lines 284-287) still fires for opposite-tail effects. In legitimate use, resamples are re-draws of the same statistic that produced `observed`, so they normally cluster near `observed` and the wrong reference rarely changes the verdict — the failure needs pathologically clustered resamples sitting just past the null median. But it is a real integrity hole in a verdict-gating check, and it is completely untested, so it could silently regress further. MEDIUM is well-calibrated; not HIGH because exploitation requires degenerate inputs and the verdict is still partially protected.

Not a false positive, not already handled, not documented-out-of-scope: the docstrings actively assert the stronger property that the code fails to provide.

Suggested fix is sound: anchor stability to the observed effect — e.g. require the fraction of resamples whose own empirical p in the predicted tail stays <= alpha (or that fall on the observed side of the null) to meet stability_threshold.
input.evidence:
PoC 1 (reproduces the reported false SUPPORTED), run with /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python:
  null = list(range(0,21))   # median 10
  observed = 100             # p_support = 0.0455 (significant)
  resamples = [10.0001]*100  # just above null median, far below observed 100
  confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=100, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=resamples, stability_threshold=0.9)
  => Verdict label SUPPORTED, evidence stability=1.0, p_support=0.0455, p_contradict=1.0
The resamples reproduce nothing of the size-100 effect yet the verdict is SUPPORTED at stability 1.0.

PoC 2 (shows the reference-point bug directly):
  _resample_stability([10.0001]*100, median(null)=10, "greater") = 1.0   # current behavior
  _resample_stability([10.0001]*100, observed=100, "greater") = 0.0      # if anchored to observed
  fraction of those resamples significant at alpha=0.05 vs null = 0.0    # suggested-fix criterion
  Same degenerate resamples score 1.0 under the current rule and 0.0 under either fix variant.

Test gap confirmed: tests/test_confirm.py:142-143 define _STABLE_HI=[100.0]*20 and _STABLE_LO=[-100.0]*20 — all stability tests place resamples far from the null, never in the "barely past null median, far from observed" regime, so the flaw is never exercised (also tests/integration/test_methodology_e2e.py:68-71).

Source: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:179-189 (_resample_stability) and line 265 (call site passing median(null_distribution)); gating at lines 295-300.

content.text:
Verified the assessor's pass-2 finding as a TRUE production-readiness defect.

Summary: In `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py`, line 265 anchors resample "stability" to `median(null_distribution)` instead of the observed statistic. `_resample_stability` (lines 179-189) then counts resamples merely on the predicted side of the null center, so resamples that barely clear the null median but reproduce none of the observed effect score stability=1.0 and earn a SUPPORTED verdict — contradicting the docstring's claim that it measures "the effect direction reproduces across resamples."

I reproduced the assessor's exact PoC against the real code (verdict=SUPPORTED, stability=1.0 with null median 10, observed 100, resamples [10.0001]×100) and confirmed the test suite never exercises this regime (`_STABLE_HI`/`_STABLE_LO` always sit far from the null).

Severity: MEDIUM — the gate is weakened but not defeated (resamples must still be on the predicted side; the independent contradiction check still fires), and legitimate resamples normally cluster near the observed statistic. Calibration matches the original assessment.
